content.problemStatement:
Mario has reached Bowser's Castle and is inches away from rescuing Princess Peach. He has to answer the following problem at the gate to be allowed to enter. Can you help him?

Let S(M)S(M)S(M) denote the set of all prime factors of MMM.

Given two positive integers AAA and BBB, determine whether AAA is divisible by all elements in S(B)S(B)S(B).


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single line of input, AAA and BBB, as mentioned in the statement.


Output Format
For each test case, print `YES` if AAA is divisible by all elements in S(B)S(B)S(B) and `NO` otherwise.


You may print each character of the string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings `YES`, `yEs`, `yes`, and `yeS` will all be treated as identical).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers A and B (1 ≤ A, B ≤ 10^18).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if A is divisible by every prime factor of B, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: B=63 has prime factors {3,7}. Both divide A=21 → YES.\nTest case 2: B=72 has prime factors {2,3}. Both divide A=72 → YES.\nTest case 3: B=20 has prime factors {2,5}. A=25 is not divisible by 2 → NO.
When B=1 it has no prime factors, condition is vacuously true → YES.\nB=49 has prime factor {7} and 7 divides A=7 → YES.
B=13 is prime and does not divide A=6 → NO.\nB=12 has prime factors {2,3}; both divide A=30 → YES.

solution.approach: For each test case we only need to know whether every prime that divides B also divides A. Let g = gcd(A, B). All prime factors of B that also divide A are contained in g. We can repeatedly remove from B the common part g: while g > 1 { B /= g; g = gcd(A, B); }. After the loop, if B becomes 1 then every prime factor of the original B was removed, meaning each of them divides A → answer YES; otherwise some prime factor remained → answer NO. This uses only the Euclidean algorithm and runs in O(log min(A,B)) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * log(min(A, B))) – each gcd computation is logarithmic and the loop runs at most the number of distinct prime factors of B (≤ 60 for 64‑bit numbers).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few 64‑bit variables are used.
